Consider reading one more document. . . misinformation_ detector 2 read docs with conflicting years or contradiction phrases [CROSS-CHECK WARNING] Your read documents may contain conflicting information. . . Table 9: Code PFs satisfying criterion A+B (prompt-equivalent), 2 of 12 skills. Both have empty intervene bodies that emit only NOOP audit records; their behavioral effect comes entirely from the corresponding SKILL.md text being injected into the system prompt at episode start. Skill ID Trigger intervene behavior code_negative_ handling code_test_ walkthrough weak signal on negative / integer in question; no static check always fires on class Solution or stdin code; no static check NOOP audit only NOOP audit only non-None on the same FINAL. When the count exceeds handler_vote_threshold (default 4 for web, 3 for math), the FINAL is overridden to SEARCH; _MAX_FINAL_OVERRIDES = 1 prevents FINALSEARCHFINALSEARCH loop. Three (4) Budgets. (max_steps, prompt-level pre_final_step_threshold), _MAX_FINAL_OVERRIDES). classes of budget max_search_calls, max_read_calls, (max_skills_in_prompt, and execution-level caps coexist: episode-level timeout_seconds), max_phase_instructions, (pf_top_k, per-PF rate limits, B.7 Skill Retrieval and PF Selection Prompt When the framework retrieves PFs at the start of an episode, the teacher is asked to rank candidate skills against the current question. The selector prompt explicitly forbids over-selection (fewer well-targeted PFs is better than many irrelevant ones) and pins three mandatory PFs that the teacher cannot drop. This second stage of filtering complements the lexical scoring in SkillSelector and reduces the chance that broad or off-topic skills affect the trajectory. Self-Improving Pipeline Details The self-improving stage is the offline counterpart to inference: where inference runs the agent against fixed library, self-improvement grows that library. Each epoch revisits residual failures from the current checkpoint and runs an eight-phase pipeline (Phases AH). The phases produce per-epoch artifacts under output_dir/epoch_{i}/: trajectories, failure analyses, candidate skills, validation reports, teacher reviews, gradient streams, and training-data files. Algorithm 1 summarizes one epoch. Algorithm 1: One self-improving epoch: from residual failures to library update and PF-derived training data. Input: Student policy πθ; teacher ; current library Mr; seed split Dseed; validation split Dval; budgets Kcand, ηexec, ηteach. Output: Updated library Mr+1; training datasets DSFT, DDPO. // Phase A: PF-aware rollout on the seed split 1 Traj ; 2 for Dseed do 3 τx Rollout(πθ, x, Mr, ); Traj Traj {τx}; 4 // Phase B: heuristic + LLM failure analysis 5 {τ Traj : EM(τ ) = 0}; 6 HeuristicDetect(F) LLMSummarize(F, ); 7 Cluster(P) ; // Jaccard 0.5 dedup; drop buckets < // Phase C: candidate proposal (rate-limited to Kcand) 8 Scand ; 9 for TopByFreq(C, Kcand) do 10 (md, code) ProposePF(πθ, c, Mr); Scand Scand {(md, code)}; 11 // Phases & E: executable validation and teacher review 12 Sok ; 13 for Scand do Qexec ExecValidate(s) ; return-type (Qconcept, Qtrig, Qint, Qskill 0.25Qconcept + 0.20Qtrig + 0.20Qint + 0.20Q if Qexec ηexec and Qskill ηteach then exec, Qval) TeacherReview(T , s); exec + 0.15Qval; // syntax / interface / mock-exec / Sok Sok {s}; 16 17 18 // Phase F: library update with versioning + size cap 19 Mr+1 Admit(Mr, Sok); // Phases & H: signal scoring and training-data construction 20 for τ Traj, step τ do 21 zt ScoreSignals(τ, t) ; // ( timing, modality, correctness, outcome ) At 0.15zT 22 23 (DSFT, DDPO) BuildTrainingData(Traj, {At}, Sok); 24 return Mr+1, DSFT, DDPO; + 0.50zO ; + 0.25zC + 0.10zM 18 C.1 Per-Epoch Phase Sequence Phase executes the current student against the seed split with PFs active; an optional two-stage prefilter cheaply identifies failing samples first, then concentrates the expensive PF-aware rollout on those. Phase clusters failures (C.2). Phase asks the student to author candidate skills against each large/novel cluster, capped at 5 candidates per epoch (C.3). Phase runs four executable checks on each candidate (C.4). Phase asks the teacher for five-dimensional review (C.5). Phase admits accepted candidates to the library with versioned __v{N} suffix (C.6). Phase assigns PF-mediated credit to produce student and teacher gradient streams. Phase emits SFT/DPO training data over both objectives (action-correction and skill-authoring). The validation pool is built once at setup time from the tail of each test dataset (samples after the test boundary), shuffled with fixed seed, and split into 50 seed and 50 val samples per dataset, yielding 250 + 250 samples across the five active web datasets. Cached splits are reused across runs unless the seed budget is set to 1 (use all). C.2 Phase Failure Signal Detection The heuristic detector applies twelve handPhase uses two detectors in parallel. including premature_final (trajectory coded rules against every failed trajectory, than three steps), repeated_search (queries with token-Jaccard > 0.8), shorter no_read_before_final, query_too_broad/too_narrow, wrong_entity_focus (answer/gold token overlap < 30%), reasoning_hallucination (final whose content tokens are < 30% grounded in read content), format_mismatch, partial_answer, contradictory_evidence_ignored, and pf_override_harmful (a PF rewrote the action but the episode still failed). excessive_steps_no_progress, The LLM detector asks the teacher to abstract each failed trajectory into reusable category (Prompt C.1); per-trajectory generations run with temperature=0.3 and max_tokens=400, and concurrency is bounded by thread pool. Heuristic and LLM patterns are deduplicated independently (token-Jaccard 0.5 within the same category) and then bucketed into six broad existing-skill groups for novelty scoring; clusters with novelty 0.3 are flagged is_new_category and prioritized for proposal. Buckets below min_cluster_size (= 3) are dropped. Prompt C.1: Phase LLM Failure Summarizer SYSTEM: You are an expert failure analyst for ReAct web-search agent. For each failed QA trajectory you must produce concise, reusable abstraction of WHY the agent failed so that new Program Function (PF) skill can be authored to fix this class of failure. USER (per trajectory): Question: {question[:500]} Gold answer(s): {gold} Agents final answer: {pred[:200]} Trajectory (truncated, one line per step): {trace[:1800]} Return STRICT JSON with keys: category: short snake_case category id (4-30 chars, e.g. constraint_leak, premature_summary, stale_evidence). Reuse an existing id if the failure mode matches. abstraction: 1-sentence description of the recurring failure mode. trigger: when in ReAct step this failure manifests. intervention_hint: what PF should do to prevent this. Output ONLY the JSON object. C.3 Phase Skill Proposal For each surviving cluster, the student model authors candidate skill consisting of (i) SKILL.md document with YAML frontmatter, trigger conditions, intervention design, and concrete examples; and (ii) ProgramFunction subclass implementing should_activate/intervene. The 19 proposer prompt (Prompt C.2) constrains the keyword arguments of Intervention and forbids LLM calls inside should_activate, so the validators mock execution remains feasible. Generation runs at temperature=0.7, capped at five candidates per epoch. Prompt C.2: Phase Skill Proposer (system, excerpt) You are skill designer for ReAct web search agent. The agent operates in SEARCH -> READ -> FINAL loop. At each step, Program Functions (PFs) can check conditions and intervene by modifying the agents action or injecting context. Your job: given cluster of recurring failures, propose NEW skill that will prevent this failure pattern in the future. The skill consists of: 1. SKILL.md specification (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). 2. ProgramFunction Python class with should_activate() and intervene() methods. IMPORTANT RULES: - The PF must be deterministic (NO LLM calls in should_activate). - should_activate receives: step_context (dict), action_type (str: "SEARCH"/"READ"/"FINAL"), arg (str). - intervene receives the same + optional teacher model, and returns an Intervention. - The PF class must inherit from ProgramFunction and use the @register_pf decorator. should_activate must return bool; intervene must return an Intervention object. EXACT Intervention interface (use ONLY these keyword arguments): type: InterventionType new_action_type: Optional[str] new_action_arg: Optional[str] context_text: reason: skill_id: str str str (NOOP MODIFY_ACTION INJECT_CONTEXT) # "SEARCH" "READ" "FINAL" C.4 Phase Executable Validation Each candidate is wrapped in sandbox preamble (mocked InterventionType, Intervention, ProgramFunction, and register_pf) and subjected to four sequential checks: syntax (ast.parse), interface (a class inheriting from ProgramFunction with the two correctly-signed methods), mock execution (3 mock step contexts {SEARCH, READ, FINAL} = 9 invocations), and return type (intervene constructs an Intervention). The three mock contexts cover the early-step/no-read regime (step 1, no reads), the mid-step/has-read regime (step 5, two reads), and the late-step/empty-results-with-contradictions regime (step 12). Any runtime exception fails check three; Q_exec < 0.3 on review is later treated as hard reject. C.5 Phase Teacher Five-Dimensional Review The teacher receives the candidate as four blocks (header, trigger conditions, intervention description, full SKILL.md and PF code) plus the source failure clusters metadata, and is asked to score five dimensions on [0, 1]: concept, trigger, intervention, executability, and validation utility. The composite is Qskill = 0.25 Qconcept + 0.20 Qtrigger + 0.20 Qintervene + 0.20 Qexec + 0.15 Qval. The decision is taken in priority order: hard reject if Qexec < 0.3; otherwise the teachers literal DECISION line if present; otherwise threshold fallback (Qskill 0.60 accept; 0.42 revise; else reject). Two further thresholds, new_group_threshold = 0.75 and same_group_threshold = 0.60, gate library admission for entirely new categories versus refinement of an existing one. The system and user prompts are reproduced in Prompts C.3C.4. Prompt C.3: Phase Reviewer (system) You are an expert skill quality reviewer for ReAct web search agent system. 20 The agent uses Program Functions (PFs) -- deterministic Python hooks that run every step. Each PF has: - should_activate(step_context, action_type, arg) -> bool - intervene(step_context, action_type, arg, teacher=None) -> Intervention Intervention types: MODIFY_ACTION (change action), INJECT_CONTEXT (add text to observation), NOOP. step_context fields: question, step_count, has_read, search_count, read_count, empty_results, contradictory_sources, max_steps, action_history, last_search_results_text, all_read_contents, thought. You must evaluate candidate skills rigorously across 5 dimensions. Prompt C.4: Phase Reviewer (user, response template) ## Review Instructions Score each dimension from 0.0 to 1.0 and provide brief feedback. ### Dimension 1: Concept (Q_concept) - Is this real, recurrent failure type? - Is it generalizable, not single-case patch? - Is it clearly distinct from existing skills? ### Dimension 2: Trigger (Q_trigger) - Is should_activate() condition specific and deterministic? - Does it depend on available step_context fields? - Is it neither too broad (many false positives) nor too narrow? ### Dimension 3: Intervention (Q_intervene) - Does intervene() appropriately address the failure? - Could it cause harmful side effects? - Is the intervention proportional (not over-blocking)? ### Dimension 4: Executability (Q_exec) - Does the code follow the ProgramFunction interface? - Would it import without errors? Are return types correct? ### Dimension 5: Validation Utility (Q_val) - Will this likely reduce the target failure type? - Will it transfer to unseen questions? - Will it interact well with existing PFs? Respond in EXACTLY this format: Q_concept: [0.0-1.0] Concept feedback: [brief] ... (Q_trigger, Q_intervene, Q_exec, Q_val similarly) ... DECISION: [ACCEPT / REVISE / REJECT] OVERALL FEEDBACK: [1-3 sentences] C.6 Phase Library Update and Versioning Accepted candidates are appended to generated/{skill_id}__v{N}/SKILL.md, and their PF code is appended to dynamic_program_functions.py. When candidate matches an existing base id, accept rewrites it as the next version (__v{N+1}); the old version remains on disk for trajectory replay. The runtime loads only the highest version per base id (skill_ids_latest), so older versions stay auditable but never fire. max_library_size = 50 caps total active skill count, and library_history.json records every per-skill decision and review score so closedloop runs can reconstruct the gated decision tree without rerunning rollouts. C.7 Failure Clustering and Candidate PF Proposal Self-improvement begins from the residual failures of the current checkpoint. Failures are grouped into recurring failure-repair patterns using the heuristic pass followed by the LLM consolidation pass (C.2); to avoid near-duplicate skills, candidate groups are deduplicated using similaritybased clustering before proposal. Proposal is rate-limited so the library grows slowly and remains controllable over many rounds. 21 C.8 Why Strict Filtering Matters The central empirical observation behind our framework is that self-improvement is useful only when library evolution is tightly controlled. Adding more skills is not automatically beneficial. When validation and filtering are weak, the library accumulates noisy or redundant skills, retrieval precision drops, and policy quality deteriorates. When filtering is strict, each accepted PF carries clearer intervention semantics and higher reuse value, and the evolving library continues to improve the agent over time. We therefore treat self-improving PF evolution as validated and rate-limited process rather than unconstrained skill generation.

sections.title: D Additional Experimental Details
sections.content: D.1 Datasets and Train/Test Splits For web-search reasoning and mathematical reasoning, our test sets and training pools follow the protocol of AgentFlow [3]: we adopt the same source datasets, the same per-dataset test sizes, and the same convention of reserving the first samples per file as the held-out test set with the remainder forming the training pool. This makes the two domains directly comparable to AgentFlow and to the prior search/reasoning baselines listed in D. For coding, AgentFlow does not provide precedent, so we adopt the same training-data construction protocol and additionally carve out per-dataset test/training splits ourselves, as detailed below. Across all domains, training pools are used both for the bootstrap PF-aware rollouts of V1 and as the source of seed/validation samples for the closed-loop evolution loop of V2 (C). Web-search reasoning. Each source file contains 1,000 randomly sampled questions; the first 200 rows of each file constitute the test set and the remaining 800 rows constitute the training pool. The closed-loop validation pool described in is drawn from the tail of the training pool (samples after the 200-row test boundary), shuffled with seed 42 and split into 50 seed and 50 val samples per dataset. Datasets whose total size equals their test boundary are auto-skipped because no tail remains; in our default web-search configuration, this leaves five active datasets feeding the seed/val splits ( 250 seed + 250 val). Table 10: Web-search dataset sizes. Test-set selection follows AgentFlow [3]. Dataset Total Test Train pool HotpotQA [26] 2WikiMultihopQA [27] MuSiQue [28] 1,000 1,000 1,000 200 200 200 800 800 800 Mathematical reasoning. The math benchmarks are small, so we use the entire dataset as the test set, exactly as in AgentFlow [3]: AIME24 (30 problems), AMC23 (40 problems), and GameOf24 (1,362 problems). The training data follows AgentFlows training-pool composition. The closedloop validation pool of draws from the same training-pool tail under the math configuration (data_subdir = data_math), with the difficulty gate disabled because all math problems are uniformly hard. Table 11: Mathematical reasoning dataset sizes. The full set for evaluation follows AgentFlow [3]. Dataset Total Test AIME24 [29] AMC23 [30] GameOf24 [31] 30 40 1, full full full Coding. Coding is the only domain not covered by AgentFlow [3], so we constructed the train/test splits explicitly. The training-data construction protocol (PF-aware rollout, four-signal scoring, V1/V2 topology) is identical to the other two domains. For HumanEval+ and MBPP+, we use the EvalPlus extended versions and reserve the first 50 unique problems (100 entries after EvalPlus row expansion) 22 as the test set, with the remaining unique problems forming the training pool. For BigCodeBench, we reserve 100 entries as the test set and use the remaining 1,040 entries as the training pool. Full per-dataset statistics are reported in Table 12. Table 12: Coding dataset sizes. AgentFlow does not cover coding, so we constructed the splits ourselves following the same training-pool layout convention. Test is the held-out evaluation set used in Table 3; Train pool is the remainder available for PF-aware rollouts. EvalPlus-based datasets count rows (a single problem may expand into multiple test entries). Dataset Total Test Train pool HumanEval+ MBPP+ BigCodeBench/Full BigCodeBench/Hard 328 (164 unique) 756 (378 unique) 1,140 148 100 (50 unique) 100 (50 unique) 100 full 228 (114 unique) 656 (328 unique) 1,040 Self-improving seed/val split (closed-loop only). Both V2 closed-loop training (E4E6) and the offline self-improving runs of draw their evolve-time seed and validation samples from the tail of the training pool described above, shuffled deterministically with seed 42. The default budget is 50 seed + 50 val per dataset, capped at half the available tail for any dataset whose tail is smaller than 100. Datasets with no tail (i.e. test set already covers the entire file) are auto-skipped at setup time. Setting the seed budget to 1 uses the entire tail, with no validation split; this is reserved for full-coverage failure summarization in late evolve cycles. D.2 Detailed Definition of E1E6 The six post-training settings form 2 3 grid over loop topology and training method. V1: Open-loop training. V1 trains on fixed PF-aware rollout pool collected from frozen skill library. The library snapshot is fixed throughout training and no additional skill evolution occurs after data collection. V2: Closed-loop training. V2 periodically evolves the skill library and refreshes the training data accordingly. This creates closed loop in which newly accepted PFs can affect both subsequent rollouts and subsequent training data. Training methods. The three training methods are supervised fine-tuning (SFT), rejection sampling (RS), and on-policy distillation (OPD). Their Cartesian product with V1/V2 gives: E1: V1 open-loop SFT; E2: V1 open-loop RS; E3: V1 open-loop OPD; E4: V2 closed-loop SFT; E5: V2 closed-loop RS; E6: V2 closed-loop OPD. In E3 and E6, GPT-4o serves as the teacher. D.3 Shared Training Configuration Table 13 summarizes the core training configuration shared across E1E6. Across all settings, we use LoRA with rank 16 and α = 32, bf16 precision, and gradient checkpointing. Effective tokens per optimizer step are 65K, so each epoch is small number of optimizer steps even with the full SFT pool, which is why SFT runs for 10 epochs and RS / closed-loop runs use 8 epochs per phase. Table 13: Training configuration for E1E6. All models use Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct with LoRA, bf16 training, and the same maximum sequence length. Setting Loop Method LR Epochs Rollout setup E1 E2 E3 E4 E5 E6 Open-loop Open-loop Open-loop Closed-loop Closed-loop Closed-loop SFT RS OPD SFT RS OPD 1 105 5 106 5 106 1 105 5 106 5 106 10 8 per iteration 10 refreshed per phase 8 per phase 5 per phase = 2, temp 0.9, top-p 0.95, max steps 5 same as E2 refreshed after evolution same as E2 same as E2 Teacher GPT-4o GPT-4o 23 D.4 Rollout and Teacher Configuration For rejection sampling and on-policy distillation, the rollout configuration is shared unless otherwise noted. We use group size 2, sampling temperature 0.9, top-p 0.95, maximum reasoning steps 5, and maximum search calls 3. In OPD, GPT-4o serves as the teacher with maximum response length 512 and concurrency 64. Every rollout-using experiment runs in teacher-snippet mode: the teacher returns 200-token snippet per SEARCH and READ is disabled (max_read_calls = 0); this decouples training rollouts from SerpAPI quotas and READ-cost variance, at the cost of trusting the teacher to summarize. Because of teacher-side 429 noise, the RS filter accepts min_score = 0.15 on composite 0.7EM+0.2F1+0.1StepEconomy rather than require_exact_match=true. D.5 Closed-Loop Evolution Modes Closed-loop training supports two evolution modes. Full evolution. The full mode runs the complete self-improving pipeline (Phases AH). One evolve cycle takes approximately one hour on the canonical setup; only E4 uses it, because SFT can amortize the cost over the slower 8-epoch phase. Lite evolution. The lite mode samples 20 failed trajectories from the rollout pool, asks the current student vLLM checkpoint for at most three skill proposals, applies ast.parse compile-checks and skill-id deduplication, and writes the result to generated/ with no teacher API calls and no clustering, review, or gradient computation. It is significantly cheaper (5% of the wall-clock) and yields most of the library-growth benefit, so E5 and E6 use it under their faster three-epoch phases. D.6 Data Construction Pipeline Training data is built in four layers. Layer 0 produces raw trajectories: V1 runs one-shot PF-aware ReAct rollout over the training pool with skills, PFs, and teacher all enabled (effectively Phase run once); V2 re-runs Phase every closed-loop cycle on the current student checkpoint with the just-evolved library. Layer 1 scores each step with the four-signal aggregator and filters by 0.25 reward floor; both the fine 15-dim breakdown and the coarse 4-scalar view are persisted on every sample so an offline re-scorer can re-weight without rerunning rollouts. Layer 2 renders steps into one of two objectives: Objective (use PFs) emits SFT, DPO, and prompt-only files where the SFT target is the PF-corrected action, the DPO chosen/rejected pair is restricted to steps with was_modified=True, and sample_weight carries the per-step reward; Objective (evolve) emits SFT and DPO files for the skill-author task, with target equal to the candidates SKILL.md + PF code and reward rskill = Qskill + 0.5 EMval. Layer 3 is the per-experiment YAML data: block: each points at one of the Layer-2 jsonls, and V2 experiments add refresh hook that rebuilds the file in-place between phases. D.7 Post-Training Recipes We study three post-training settings. SFT. PF-mediated corrections are converted into weighted supervised targets. This is the simplest way to internalize repaired actions. Rejection sampling. Trajectories are sampled, scored by both task success and PF-conditioned intervention quality, and only the top subset is retained. This selects trajectories that are both correct and behaviorally clean. On-policy distillation. The current model generates its own trajectories, PFs intervene on difficult states, and the corrected behaviors are distilled back into the model. This aligns the training distribution with the models own inference-time state distribution. The three recipes are deliberately defined over the same signal interface so that recipe choice and signal ablation remain orthogonal experimental axes. 24 Fine-Grained Signal Composition The main text uses four coarse signals (timing, modality, correctness, outcome) with weights (0.15, 0.10, 0.25, 0.50). Internally, each is computed as weighted sum of finer sub-signals; the coarse formulation is used in training because it is stable and easy to ablate, while the fine decomposition is used for diagnostics and analysis. Timing (S1). step is defined to be risky on the proposed action (before any PF intervention) if it is FINAL with no prior READ, FINAL at step_count < 3, or SEARCH after empty_results. The four sub-signals are s1.tp (+0.25, risky and any PF activated), s1.fp (0.10, not risky but PF still activated), s1.fn (0.10, risky but no PF activated), and s1.phase (+0.05, normalized step index, rewarding earlier interventions). Modality (S2). s2.pre_action (+0.35, MODIFY_ACTION fired before the action ran) and s2.post_obs (+0.35, an INJECT_CONTEXT fired after the observation) carry all current signal; s2.pre_reasoning and s2.post_action are reserved slots that return 0 until corresponding intervention types are added, kept registered with non-zero weights so future PFs accrue reward without changing config. s3.syntactic (+0.20, action type valid and argument non-empty); Correctness (S3). s3.semantic (+0.50, teacher-judged step score with heuristic fallback that gives 0.7 to FINALREAD rescue, 0.5 to other modifications, 0.3 otherwise); s3.domain (+0.30, plugin slot averaged over domain-specific quality functions registered under s3.domain.<name>), default 0. Outcome (S4). s4.local (+0.40, scored by transition: 0.8 for FINALREAD, 0.7 for FINALSEARCH, 0.5 for SEARCHSEARCH rewrite, 0.3 for any other modification, else 0); s4.downstream (+0.40, episode EM); s4.cost (0.10, clip((T 15)/10, 0, 1)); s4.side_effect (0.10, modified action but episode still failed). S4 is the heaviest family in coarse mode because it is the only one anchored directly to ground-truth EM. Aggregation. In fine mode, the per-step score is (cid:80) sub wsub valuesub, optionally normalized by (cid:80) wsub. In coarse mode, each family is first reduced to scalar via family-internal normalization (preserving the sign of penalty sub-signals), then combined with the four coarse weights. The episodelevel reward is 50/50 blend of the per-step mean and the terminal EM, so perfect-EM episode with poor PF behavior does not get full credit and near-miss episode with strong intermediate signals is not completely discarded. Skill-level reward. For training the skill author (Phase H), there is one additional skill-level reward, rskill = Qskill + λ EMval with default λ = 0.5, where EMval is the change in validation EM after the skill is added. This is the only signal that is not step-level.

sections.title: F Additional Main Results
sections.content: This section provides three complementary views of the main results: (i) controlled stage-by-stage breakdown that isolates inference-time intervention from post-training internalization and from closed-loop evolution; (ii) deeper read of the cross-domain transfer to coding; and (iii) the dynamics of the skill library across self-improvement rounds. F.1 Stage-by-Stage Contribution Table 14 is constructed by selectively enabling each stage of the framework while keeping all other knobs fixed at the defaults from D. Adding inference-time PFs to the multi-loop baseline already accounts for the bulk of the gain on web-search reasoning, since PFs convert recurring decision-level failures into structured repairs without modifying the policy. Post-training then internalizes those repairs into the models own state distribution and captures the additional gain that PF-conditioned supervision provides after the policy has been queried with the library. Closed-loop evolution layers on top, growing the library when residual failures expose patterns that the seed library does not cover. The composition is roughly additive but sub-linear: the stages are not independent, and evolution alone contributes little when PFs are not also active during inference. 25 Table 14: Stage-by-stage contribution of the three mechanisms in our framework on web-search reasoning average accuracy. Each row turns on one additional component while keeping all others at the defaults from D. Setting Avg. Acc. Base multi-loop agent (RA-Agent) + Inference-time PF intervention + Post-training internalization (E3, OPD) + Closed-loop evolution (E5, RS) Full system (best per-recipe combination) 31.2 56.2 62.5 60.3 F.2 Detailed Coding Results In Section 4, we report pass@1 across HumanEval(Base/Plus), MBPP(Base/Plus), and BigCodeBench(Full/Hard). The coding setting is the strictest cross-domain transfer test for our framework: the action space collapses to single FINAL emission, READ is unavailable, and the only PF surface is regex-based static analysis on the emitted code body that returns either an INJECT_CONTEXT hint or NOOP audit record (B.4). Despite this constrained interface, two patterns are visible in the table. Inference-time PFs help most on edge-case-heavy benchmarks. Static checks for missing length guards, off-by-one indexing, and stdin parsing are concentrated in the harder splits (BigCodeBench/Hard, MBPP/Plus), so the inference-time PF rows tend to gain more there than on HumanEval/Base, where the Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct backbone already scores 81.7%. The phaseinstruction surface (+code_test_walkthrough, +code_edge_cases) is the dominant lever in this domain the PF code itself is auditing rather than rewriting. Internalization complements RL-style coders. GRPO [19] and KodCode-RL [39] are stronger overall on HumanEval and MBPP because they expand the policy frontier through reward-shaped exploration. Our PF-derived supervision, in contrast, primarily targets capability elicitation: by training on PF-corrected steps, it lifts the rate at which existing strategies are reliably executed without performing exploration-style reward search. The two directions are largely orthogonal, and our coding gains are concentrated on edge-case correctness rather than on raw algorithmic difficulty. F.3 Per-Dataset Robustness The web-search and math averages reported in the main text aggregate over heterogeneous benchmarks, and the per-dataset numbers in Section 4 make few qualitative points worth recording. On web-search, PFs help most on MuSiQue, where multi-hop chains create many opportunities for entity confusion and premature FINAL exactly the failure modes that retrieval_failure, wrong_entity_confusion, and insufficient_exploration target. 2Wiki sits in the middle: AgentFlow remains stronger there, plausibly because its RL-style policy search compensates for the relatively shorter retrieval chains where PF-style local repair has less leverage. On math, PFs are most effective on AMC23 and GameOf24, where small algebraic and arithmetic slips dominate failure cases (addressed by algebraic_sign_error, arithmetic_slip, verification_missing). AIME24 remains the hardest: failures there are not local slips but missed strategy choices, which is why broader RL-style exploration outperforms our elicitationoriented framework on that dataset. F.4 Evolution Dynamics Across Rounds We track three quantities across self-improvement rounds for each domain: the per-round success rate of candidate proposals (the fraction passing the executable validation pipeline of C.4), the number of skills accepted into the library after teacher review (C.5), and the cumulative library size (C.6). The dynamics support three observations. First, the acceptance rate stays high in early rounds and decays gradually as the seed library already covers the most frequent residual failures, indicating that the system does not over-grow the library when easy failures have been absorbed. Second, the library size stays well below the hard cap max_library_size = 50 even by the final round; the 26 bottleneck is novelty rather than capacity. Third, web-search and math show qualitatively similar curves, while coding accepts fewer skills per round because most of its PFs reduce to audit NOOPs and the proposer is conservative about adding new audit hooks. The same dynamics, at finer per-skill granularity, are reflected in the review-score histogram in Appendix I: skills accepted in later rounds tend to receive higher Q_concept but lower Q_trigger, consistent with later proposals being conceptually clearer but harder to fire reliably.

sections.title: G Signal and Filtering Ablations
sections.content: Table 5 in the main text bundles three ablations of the framework into single panel. This appendix gives the precise definition of each row so that the reader can reproduce or re-interpret any cell. The panel consists of three blocks that share the same backbone (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct), the same web-search rollout configuration of D, and the same evaluation pool (HotpotQA / 2Wiki / MuSiQue): an inference-time component ablation, signal ablation on the closed-loop run E5, and filtering ablation on the same E5 run. Across all rows, columns T, M, C, indicate whether the timing, modality, correctness, and outcome supervision signals were active during training, and Exec, Teach indicate whether executable validation and teacher review were applied during library evolution. check mark means the component was active; dash means it was disabled; N/A means the column does not apply to that block. Table 15 summarizes the four-signal interface and its default weights, which serve as the canonical reference for the columns of the signal ablation. Table 15: Four core supervision signals for skill-conditioned intervention. Each signal aggregates multiple low-level cues and captures distinct aspect of PF-mediated policy improvement. Signal Meaning Composition Timing (tt) Modality (mt) Correctness (qt) Quality of correction Outcome (ot) When to intervene How to intervene Whether it helps s1.tp, s1.fp, s1.fn, s1.phase s2.pre_action, s2.post_obs s3.syntactic, s3.semantic, s3.domain s4.local, s4.downstream, s4.cost, s4.side_effect Weight 0.15 0.10 0.25 0.50 G. Inference-Time Component Ablation The first block of Table 5 isolates the inference-time contribution of PF-style state-to-intervention interventions versus an external teacher model. All four supervision signals are present in this block by definition (the rollout always sees complete observation/action stream); the Exec/Teach columns are N/A because no library evolution is run. Full. Both PFs and the teacher are active. PFs intervene at decision time using the four-signalconditioned policy from B.4, and the teacher participates wherever PF or handler delegates to it (e.g. retrieval_failure query rewriting, reasoning_error CORRECT/WRONG judging, FINAL-time handler verifiers from B.6). This row reproduces the inference-only result from the main text (56.2% average). RA-Agent (multi-loop). Both PFs and the teacher are disabled; the agent runs the same multi-step ReAct loop without any state-to-intervention machinery. This row is the lower bound and reproduces the multi-loop baseline from the main text (31.2%). Prompt-Only Skills. All skills are presented purely as text, without executable intervention. PF only. PFs are active but the teacher is unavailable. PFs that declare needs_teacher = True (retrieval_failure, format_extraction_error, reasoning_error, answer_confidence_guard) fall back to their deterministic code-only paths (e.g. regex-based query shortening rather than teacher-paraphrased queries); FINAL-time LLM-assisted handlers are skipped. This row tests how much of the gain comes from the executable PF substrate alone. The only. Teacher disabled is (enable_program_functions=false). The teacher can still answer through the multi-loop ReAct trace and through outer-loop format post-processing / multi-round retry from B.6, but no state-to-intervention object intercepts decision points. This row tests how much of the gain comes from simply having access to stronger model rather than from PF-mediated control. available teacher PFs but are 27 G.2 Signal Ablation The second block of Table 5 evaluates the contribution of each of the four supervision signals. All rows in this block use the closed-loop rejection sampling experiment E5 (V2 RS, D) and full filtering (Exec + Teach), so the only knob that varies is which subset of {T, M, C, O} enters the per-step weight At = λttt + λmmt + λqqt + λoot used in E. All four signals. (0.15, 0.10, 0.25, 0.50). Reference row at 60.3%. The default E5 run, with the canonical weights (λt, λm, λq, λo) = w/o Timing. The timing signal tt is dropped: the per-step score no longer rewards firing on risky steps (FINAL with no read, FINAL at low step count, SEARCH after empty results) or punishes firing on safe ones. The remaining three signals are renormalized to sum to 1. The drop of 7.8 points indicates that, although timing carries the smallest default weight, removing it lets the policy intervene at the wrong steps and miss risky ones, both of which propagate into worse downstream outcomes. w/o Modality. The modality signal mt is dropped: the framework no longer distinguishes MODIFY_ACTION (pre-action) from INJECT_CONTEXT (post-observation) interventions. The drop of 15.5 points is the largest, which we read as evidence that how an intervention fires matters as much as when: rewriting SEARCH query and injecting reading reminder are not interchangeable, and model trained without modality credit learns to treat them as such. w/o Correctness. The correctness signal qt is dropped: the resulting executed action is no longer scored for syntactic validity, semantic appropriateness, or domain consistency. The drop of 12.1 points reflects that without local correctness, the policy retains interventions that were locally invalid (e.g. malformed SEARCH argument) as long as the trajectory eventually succeeded. w/o Outcome. The outcome signal ot is dropped: the per-step score no longer ties intervention quality to downstream EM, cost, or side-effects. The drop of 12.8 points reflects that without outcome credit, the model can over-internalize PF-aligned trajectories that look right locally but do not improve final answers. The four drops together support the frameworks central claim that PF-derived supervision is irreducible to single scalar: each of when, how, whether well-formed, and whether it paid off contributes distinct 715 points to the closed-loop run. G.3 Filtering Ablation The third block of Table 5 evaluates the effect of admission control during library evolution. All rows use the same E5 closed-loop rejection sampling configuration with all four signals active; the only knob that varies is which of executable validation (Exec) and teacher review (Teach) gates candidate skill before it is appended to the library (C.4C.6). Evolution, full filtering. Both Exec and Teach are required: candidate must pass the four executable checks of C.4 and reach Qskill 0.60 on the five-dimensional teacher review of C.5. Reference row at 60.3%. No evolution. Library evolution is turned off entirely; the run is otherwise identical to E5, but the library remains the seed snapshot throughout training. This is effectively the V1 RS analogue (E2) measured under the same protocol. At 59.3%, only 1.0 point below full evolution, this row shows that closed-loop evolution is genuine but modest contributor on top of strong fixed library. Evolution, no filtering. Evolution is turned on but neither Exec nor Teach is enforced every candidate proposed in Phase is appended to the library without any gate. The drop of 24.0 points is the central evidence for the memory-pollution hypothesis: unfiltered candidates include skills that fail to import, skills that fire too aggressively, and skills that conflict with existing PFs, and the resulting noise in the retrieval pool degrades downstream policy decisions even when the rollout configuration is otherwise unchanged. This row is also the only one in the panel that falls below the multi-loop baseline of the first block (36.3 vs. 31.2), showing that bad library evolution can be actively harmful rather than merely useless. Evolution, exec-only. Only the executable validation gate of C.4 is applied; teacher review is skipped, so syntactically valid but conceptually weak or overly specific candidates are admitted. 28 The drop of 11.5 points indicates that the executable gate alone catches the catastrophic interface failures (which is why this row is far above no filtering) but is not enough to keep retrieval precision high, because the library accumulates plausible-looking but uninformative skills. Evolution, teacher-only. Only the teacher review gate of C.5 is applied; the four executable checks are skipped, so candidates that the teacher rates highly on concept but cannot actually run end up in the library. The drop of 13.1 points shows that the teachers conceptual judgments are insufficient on their own: candidate with well-articulated concept but malformed intervene method still emits runtime errors that disable the surrounding skill at inference, and the resulting interface inconsistency erodes the gains from healthy skills. Together, the four non-default rows trace clean ordering: full filtering (60.3) > no evolution (59.3) > exec-only (48.8) > teacher-only (47.2) > no filtering (36.3). Both gates are necessary: executable validation prevents broken or ill-typed skills, teacher review filters conceptually weak ones, and removing either gate falls back to regime where the noise added by evolution outweighs the benefit of new skills. The pattern justifies our policy of treating self-improving PF evolution as validated and rate-limited process rather than unconstrained skill generation. Figure 5: Optimization diagnostics for the six post-training settings. Left: gradient norm as function of processed training tokens. Right: learning rate as function of processed training tokens. These curves help distinguish genuine differences in training dynamics from artifacts of optimization instability or scheduler choice, and provide additional context for the phase transitions observed in the main training plots. Figure 6: Training dynamics of all six post-training settings plotted against global step. From left to right, we report training loss, correction-aligned token accuracy, and policy entropy. This figure complements Figure 3 by showing that the qualitative differences across recipes remain visible under step-based view rather than only under token normalization. G.4 Case Study To complement the aggregated recovery statistics in Table 16, this section walks through one concrete trajectory per domain in which the baseline (RA-Agent multi-loop) fails and our PF-augmented agent succeeds. For each case we render the full ReAct rollout: at every step we show the agents internal thought, the proposed action, the observation returned, and (for our agent) every PF activation event with its trigger, intervention type, and the before/after action or context. The intent is to make the 29 Figure 7: Open-loop training dynamics under fixed PF library. We compare supervised fine-tuning, rejection sampling, and on-policy distillation in terms of training loss, correction-aligned token accuracy, and policy entropy as functions of processed training tokens. By removing closed-loop evolution from the comparison, this figure isolates how the three post-training recipes differ when trained on the same fixed PF-aware rollout pool. Figure 8: Closed-loop training dynamics under evolving PF libraries. We compare supervised fine-tuning, rejection sampling, and on-policy distillation in terms of training loss, correction-aligned token accuracy, and policy entropy as functions of processed training tokens. The vertical dotted marker indicates the first refresh point of the closed-loop pipeline. This figure highlights how evolving PF supervision changes the optimization trajectory relative to the fixed-library setting. corrective mechanism visible in the policy loop, not just in the aggregated statistics. All cases use Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct with the rollout configuration of D; the trajectories are taken verbatim from the evaluation logs. Table 16: Recovered failure cases and their corresponding corrective mechanisms. Percentages are computed over recovered cases only. The largest gains come from resolving entity confusion and improving search under insufficient exploration, followed by corrected reasoning and deeper reading. Failure Type Corrective Mechanism Coverage (%) resolved_entity entity_conf retrieval + insuff_explore better_search reasoning read_comp format hallucination corrected_reasoning deeper_reading improved_format 3743 2933 15 89 5 <1 Web-search case: HotpotQA multi-hop entity resolution The question is three-hop query (Walton family member died after John in 2005 her husband). Both agents use the same SEARCH/READ/FINAL action space and the same teacher-augmented retriever, but only our agent decomposes the query, forces READ before FINAL, and verifies completeness. Baseline trajectory (incorrect) Q: Who was the husband of the prominent Walton family member who died after John died in 2005? 30 Gold: Sam Walton ==== ReAct loop (RA-Agent multi-loop, no PFs, no skills) ==== [step 0] THINK "John Walton is the son of Sam Walton, the Wal-Mart founder. He died in 2005. The prominent Walton family member who died after John is likely Alice Walton or Helen Walton. Let me check." ACTION OBS SEARCH("Walton family members died after 2005") John T. Walton ; June 27, 2005 ... doc_0 John Walton, billionaire ... died Monday in plane crash doc_1 Wal-Mart Stores ... heir Sam Walton family ... doc_2 [no READ; no decomposition; agent commits to guess] [step 1] THINK "Alice Walton is the most prominent. Let me check if she died." ACTION OBS SEARCH("Alice Walton death date") doc_0 Alice Louise Walton (born October 7, 1949) is an American billionaire... [she is alive] Alice Louise Walton was born on October 7, 1949... doc_1 [step 2] THINK "Search did not return death date but Alice is the only prominent Walton know." ACTION OBS SEARCH("Alice Walton death date") [identical results; no new evidence] # SAME query repeated [step 3] ANSWER REASON FINAL "Bruce Walton" "Alice Walton (born 1949) ... is still alive, but the question implies Walton family member died after John (2005). The only plausible name recall is Bruce Walton." # FABRICATED Our trajectory (correct) ==== ReAct loop with PF library (skills_top10_with_prompt) ==== [pre-rollout] PFSelector picks top-K relevant skills for this question (decompose_complex_question, retrieval_failure, insufficient_exploration, answer_completeness, ...); three mandatory PFs always included. [step 0] THINK "Multi-hop question: (1) which Walton died after 2005, (2) who was her husband. will search broader first." PROPOSED SEARCH("Walton family member died after John Walton 2005") >> [PF FIRED] decompose_complex_question -> INJECT_CONTEXT trigger : multi-hop question with insufficient sub-question coverage (matched by SkillSelector) before after : (action unchanged) : observation will be augmented with "[DECOMPOSITION HINT] This is multi-hop question. Search for each piece of information separately. Find intermediate entities first, then search for the final answer." EXECUTED OBS SEARCH("Walton family member died after John Walton 2005") doc_0 Christy Walton took her husband Johns place in the ranking after his death... John Walton, the son of Wal-Mart founder Sam Walton... died Monday in plane crash in 2005. Helen Walton ... wife of Sam Walton ... doc_1 doc_2 (+ injected DECOMPOSITION HINT) [step 1] THINK "Plan: hop-1 is to find Walton who died AFTER 2005. The hint said find intermediate entities first. Helen Walton is plausible." ACTION OBS SEARCH("Helen Walton death date") doc_0 Died, April 19, 2007 (aged 87). Bentonville, Arkansas, U.S. ... wife of Sam Walton. On April 19, 2007, after heart failure... Walton died ... at age eighty-seven. doc_1 [step 2] THINK "Helen Walton died 2007 (after 2005). Hop-2 is her husband. should READ the document to confirm." PROPOSED FINAL("Sam Walton") # premature FINAL >> [PF FIRED] insufficient_exploration -> MODIFY_ACTION trigger : action_type=FINAL with read_count=0 reason before after : "Searched but never read; forcing read" : FINAL("Sam Walton") : READ(doc_0) # action overridden EXECUTED OBS READ(doc_0) Helen Walton ... died April 19, 2007 ... was the wife of Wal-Mart founder Sam Walton ... [step 3] THINK "Confirmed by reading: Helen Walton died 2007 after John (2005), her husband was Sam Walton." PROPOSED FINAL("Sam") >> [PF FIRED] answer_completeness -> INJECT_CONTEXT trigger : multi-part question with single-token answer before after : answer="Sam" : observation augmented with "[COMPLETENESS WARNING] The question appears to have multiple parts, but your answer may be incomplete. Make sure you address all parts." ACTION REASON FINAL("Sam Walton") "Helen Walton died after John Walton (2005) and was the wife of Sam Walton. Verified through document reading." # CORRECT three distinct loop locations: triggers three PFs at in any retrieved snippet). Phase-by-phase comparison. The baselines three steps reproduce the canonical multi-step failure modes targeted by Phase (C.2): no_read_before_final (it never READs), repeated_search (steps 1 and 2 issue identical queries), and reasoning_hallucination at FINAL (the answer Bruce Walton is not present Our (i) before step 0s SEARCH, agent decompose_complex_question fires as an INJECT_CONTEXT that surfaces decomposition hint into the next observation, steering the agent toward hop-by-hop search; (ii) at step 2, the agents proposed action is FINAL with no prior READ, so insufficient_exploration fires as MODIFY_ACTION that overrides the action from FINAL("Sam Walton") to READ(doc_0) the explicit before/after pair is (action=FINAL,arg="Sam Walton") (action=READ,arg="doc_0"); (iii) at step 3, after the agent emits single-token FINAL("Sam"), answer_completeness fires as an INJECT_CONTEXT reminding the policy that the question has multiple parts, prompting the model to expand to the gold-standard form "Sam Walton". Each intervention has an inspectable trigger, an explicit before-action / after-action pair, and logged reason the trajectory itself is the audit trail. None of these three corrections is stronger model: PFs intervene on the same Qwen-7B policy that produced the baseline. Math case: AMC23 distinct-roots polynomial counting For math, the rollout is single-step (max_steps = 1, B.4), so the entire ReAct loop is one <think>/FINAL pair. Skills enter through (a) system_summary prompts injected into the system message during PFSelector stage, and (b) pre_final verifier hook on the FINAL action. Both agents see the same problem; the only difference is whether the math skill library is active. Baseline trajectory (incorrect) Q: For how many ordered pairs (a,b) of integers does the polynomial x^3 + a*x^2 + b*x + 6 Gold: have 3 distinct integer roots? ==== ReAct loop (RA-Agent, no skills) ==== [step 0] THINK "Let roots be p,q,r. By Vieta: p+q+r = -a, pq+qr+rp = b, pqr = -6. Enumerate ORDERED triples (p,q,r) with pqr=-6 and distinct: (1, 2,-3) -> (a,b) = (0,-7) (1,-2, 3) -> (a,b) = (-2,-5) (1,-1, 6) -> (a,b) = (-6,-1) (1,-1,-6) -> (a,b) = (6,-1) (1,-3, 2) -> (a,b) = (0,-7) (1,-6,-1) -> (a,b) = (6,-1) (2,-1, 3) -> (a,b) = (-4, 1) (2,-3,-1) -> (a,b) = (2,-5) (3,-1,-2) -> (a,b) = (0,-7) Each ORDERING gives unique pair (a,b)." # FALSE ACTION FINAL("9") # WRONG 32 Our trajectory (correct) ==== ReAct loop with math skill library (11 PF skills active) ==== [pre-rollout] System prompt augmented with system_summary lines from selected math skills, including: case_incompleteness overgeneralization verification_missing : "cases must be exhaustive AND mutually exclusive" : "check formula preconditions" : "plug back into the ORIGINAL equation" [step 0] THINK "Let roots be p,q,r. By Vieta: p+q+r = -a, pq+qr+rp = b, pqr = -6. Enumerate triples (p,q,r) with pqr=-6 and distinct." Initial enumeration (8 ordered triples)... >> [PF FIRED] case_incompleteness / overgeneralization trigger before : has_case_analysis = True at pre_final : draft answer counts every ordered triple as distinct (a,b) pair intervention: phase instruction injected at pre_final: "Cases must be exhaustive AND mutually exclusive. Vieta is symmetric in (p,q,r), so permutations of the SAME multiset of roots map to the SAME (a,b). Reduce orderings to multisets before counting." REVISED REASONING Multisets {p,q,r}: {1,2,-3}, {-1,1,6}, {-1,1,-6}, {-1,2,3}, {-1,-2,3}. Discard {-1,-1,6} and {-1,-1,-6}: not 3-distinct. Consolidated (a,b) pairs: (5 distinct multisets) (0,-7), (-2,-5), (2,-5), (-4,-11), (4,-11) >> [PF FIRED] verification_missing -> pre_final hook trigger intervention: substitute each candidate (a,b) back into : always (priority 0.90) x^3 + a*x^2 + b*x + 6 and confirm 3 distinct integer roots check : x^3 - 7x + 6 = (x-1)(x-2)(x+3) [OK] x^3 - 2x^2 - 5x + 6 = (x-1)(x-3)(x+2) ... 5 distinct (a,b) pairs all verified. ... ACTION FINAL("5") # CORRECT it lists nine orderings but Phase-by-phase comparison. Both agents share the same Vieta setup; the difference is structural rather than mathematical. The baselines failure is captured exactly by the heuristic detector case_incompleteness of C.2: the question counts ordered (a, b) pairs, and Vietas symmetric polynomials collapse permutations of the same root multiset onto the same (a, b). Two PFs intervene through different surfaces: case_incompleteness and overgeneralization fire jointly as pre_final phase instruction (an INJECT_CONTEXT-style intervention) that surfaces constraint reminder before the model commits its count; verification_missing (priority 0.90, always-on) fires as pre_final verification hook that forces substitution check on each candidate (a, b). The before/after for the agents draft is (count = 9 over orderings) (count = 5 over consolidated multisets, each substitution-verified). As in the search case, no stronger model is invoked the same Qwen-7B policy is steered onto self-consistent counting frame. Coding case: LiveCodeBench-easy Equally Coding rollouts use max_steps = 1 as well; PFs run as static-analysis hooks on the emitted code body and emit INJECT_CONTEXT hints rather than rewriting code. The visible repair is the agents revised solution after the static check fires. Baseline trajectory (incorrect) Problem (LiveCodeBench-easy / atcoder_abc385_a, "Equally"): Given three integers A, B, in [1, 1000], decide whether they can be partitioned into two or more groups with equal sums. Print "Yes" or "No". Sample 1: 3 8 5 Sample 2: 2 2 2 Sample 3: 1 2 4 -> Yes -> Yes -> No (groups {3,5} and {8}, sum=8) (groups {2}, {2}, {2}, sum=2) ==== Single-step ReAct (no skills) ==== 33 [step 0] THINK "[1] RESTATE: divide A,B,C into >=2 groups with equal sums. [2] FORMAT: stdin script with input()/print(). [3] BRANCHES: - sum is odd - sum == 0 - any two sum to the third - otherwise -> No -> No -> Yes -> No" ACTION FINAL(""" a, b, = map(int, input().split()) if (a + + c) % 2 == 1: print("No") elif + + == 0: print("No") elif + == or + == or + == a: print("Yes") else: print("No") """) JUDGE test 0 (3 8 5) test 1 (2 2 2) passed FAILED (3+5==8) expected Yes, got No (2+2==4 != 2; falls through) ... pass_at_1 = False # MISSED THE all-equal CASE Our trajectory (correct) ==== Single-step ReAct with code skill library (12 PFs) ==== [pre-rollout] System prompt augmented with code_edge_cases, code_test_walkthrough, code_off_by_one ... [step 0] THINK "[1] RESTATE: ... [3] BRANCHES: - all equal - sum odd - sum <= 3 - sum == 4 or 6 - any two sum to third - otherwise -> Yes -> No -> No -> Yes -> Yes -> No" <-- missed by baseline >> [PF FIRED] code_edge_cases -> INJECT_CONTEXT trigger intervention: phase instruction reminds: : FINAL with stdin parsing + branching code "Trace each sample input through your branches before FINAL. For (2,2,2): each integer can be its own group of size 1 (k = 3 groups), so the answer is Yes whenever == == c. The a+b == test does NOT cover this case." REVISED CODE ACTION FINAL(""" (the all-equal branch is added FIRST) a, b, = map(int, input().split()) if == == c: # added by code_edge_cases hint print("Yes") elif (a + + c) % 2 == 1: print("No") elif + + <= 3: print("No") elif + + == 4 or + + == 6: print("Yes") elif + == or + == or + == a: print("Yes") else: print("No") """) JUDGE test 0 (3 8 5) test 1 (2 2 2) test 2 (1 2 4) ... pass_at_1 = True passed passed passed <- recovered by all-equal branch Phase-by-phase comparison. The baselines solution misses one branch of the case analysis: when = = C, the partition into three singleton groups always works, but its disjunction a+b==c or a+c==b or b+c==a only covers two-vs-one partitions. The corresponding heuristic in Phase is partial_answer / case_incompleteness, and the runtime PF code_edge_cases catches it through static check on the FINAL code body (branching disjunction without an allequal short-circuit) and injects context hint that explicitly walks (2,2,2) through the branches. The intervention type is INJECT_CONTEXT, not MODIFY_ACTION the framework does not rewrite the students code; the policy itself adds the == == branch in response to the hint. 34 The before/after pair is (branches: {odd, zero, two-of-three}) (branches: {all-equal, odd, sum 3, sum {4, 6}, two-of-three, else}), with the only structural change being the all-equal branch added at the top.

sections.title: H Training Dynamics
sections.content: This section unpacks the training-dynamics figures, makes the data-collection protocol explicit, and reads each curve back to property of the recipe rather than treating the plots as decoration. All six runs use the same Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct LoRA fine-tuning configuration of (rank 16, α = 32, bf16, gradient checkpointing; the recipe-specific knobs (learning rate, number of epochs, rollout shape) are summarized in Table 13. H.1 Data-Collection Protocol The curves in Figures 3 and 58 are produced from Weights & Biases run logs. The trainer (TRLs SFTTrainer for E1/E4 and the rejection-sampling/distillation drivers for E2/E3/E5/E6) emits one JSON-style loss-dict per logging interval (logging_steps = 10) into output.log. Each emitted dict reports the seven scalars we plot: loss, grad_norm, learning_rate, entropy, num_tokens, mean_token_accuracy, and epoch. The three primary signals in the main-text figure correspond to (i) cross-entropy training loss, (ii) per-step correction-aligned token accuracy (the trainers mean_token_accuracy, computed over the PF-corrected target tokens, at, rather than the raw policy outputs), and (iii) on-policy generation entropy as estimated by TRL during the forward pass. Curves are smoothed with centered moving-average window of size 5 to suppress per-step noise without distorting the slow-time-scale shape. For each we plot one canonical run the longest single Wandb run available for V1, and the concatenation of iter_0 and iter_1 for the closed-loop V2 settings (the smallest sequence that captures the first evolution boundary). Concretely the canonical traces are: E1 V1 SFT for 600 optimizer steps over 10 epochs; E2 V1 RS for 1,400 optimizer steps over 8 epochs 2 RS iterations; E3 V1 OPD for 900 optimizer steps over 10 epochs; E4 V2 SFT as 820 + 180 steps across two closed-loop iterations; E5 V2 RS as 700 + 360 steps; and E6 V2 OPD as 280 + 140 steps. Token counts (num_tokens) are accumulated across iterations so the V2 X-axes remain monotone; the iter-boundary in each V2 series is rendered as vertical dotted marker in the runs own color. H.2 Optimization Diagnostics Figure 5 reports gradient norm and learning rate against processed tokens. The gradient-norm panel verifies that the loss differences across recipes are not artifacts of unstable optimization: all six runs sit in the same magnitude band ( 100 to 101) with no run-specific spikes at the iter-boundaries, ruling out the hypothesis that the small loss bumps in V2 reflect numerical issues. The learning-rate panel makes the schedule explicit: SFT uses 1 105 peak (E1, E4); RS and OPD use 5 106 (E2, E3, E5, E6) because they operate on top of an SFT-warmed policy; warmup is 3% of total tokens for all six. Closed-loop runs apply the schedule per iter rather than globally, so each V2 curve shows fresh warmup at the iter-boundary the iter-internal LR pattern is repeated, not flattened. Reading the optimization panel together with the loss panel makes clear that the recipe-level differences in convergence rate and final level reflect the objective rather than the optimizer. H.3 Loop-Topology Comparisons Three companion figures separate the data along orthogonal axes. Figure 6 replots all six runs against global step rather than tokens to confirm that recipe ordering is robust to X-axis choice: V2 runs span fewer steps but more wall-clock seconds because the evolve cycle inserts inference and skill-proposal stages between training phases. Figures 7 and 8 each show only three curves the V1-only and V2-only subsets respectively which makes the recipe-vs-recipe contrast easier to see without cross-topology visual clutter. The vertical dotted marker in Figure 8 marks the first closed-loop refresh point in each V2 run. The most informative comparison is V1 SFT (Figure 7) versus V2 SFT (Figure 8): the V1 curve is smooth, the V2 curve shows cleanly visible break at the iter-boundary, and after the break the slope flattens because the post-evolve target pool has higher accuracy targets 35 to reach. This is direct evidence that the closed-loop refresh changes the gradient signal rather than merely re-running the same data.

sections.title: I Additional Analysis of Skill Evolution
sections.content: This section reports per-domain statistics of the self-improving PF library that complement the framework definitions in Appendix C. The numbers below are read directly from the ondisk artifacts of the closed-loop runs: library_history.json for per-skill admission events, generated/{skill_id}__v{N}/metadata.json for per-skill review scores, and snapshots/epoch_{i}/ for per-epoch library state. Table 17 aggregates the corresponding web-search uplift; the per-skill review-score breakdown is given below in Table 18 (per-family) and Table 19 (per-domain admission counts). Table 17: Effect of self-improving PF evolution on the web-search benchmark. Rows correspond to skill families introduced across evolution epochs. Each epoch cell reports the gain over the non-evolving baseline as EM / MBELLM-judge. The bottom block reports average review scores of generated skills across five quality dimensions. Skill Family Evolution Epoch Avg. E1 E2 E3 E4 reasoning_hallucination +16.7 / +11.7 +11.7 / +3.3 +10.0 / +6.7 +15.0 / +11.7 +13.3 / +13.3 +13.3 / +9.3 no_read_before_final +0.4 / +0.8 1.7 / +1.7 +1.7 / +5.0 premature_final +5.0 / +1.7 +15.0 / +5.0 +13.3 / +6.7 +11.1 / +4.4 pf_override_harmful 0.8 / 0.8 wrong_entity_focus +6.7 / +5.0 1.7 / 0.0 +6.7 / +5. 0.0 / 1.7 +1.7 / 1.7 0.0 / 1.7 Dimension Score Concept 0.83 Trigger 0.75 Intervention 0.83 Executability 0.95 Validation 0. Avg. 0.83 Generated skill review scores (scale 01) I.1 Per-Family Review Scores Recall from C.5 that each candidate skill is rated by the teacher LLM on five dimensions: concept (Qconcept), trigger (Qtrigger), intervention (Qintervene), executability (Qexec), and validation utility (Qval). The composite is Qskill = 0.25Qconcept + 0.20Qtrigger + 0.20Qintervene + 0.20Qexec + 0.15Qval. Table 18 averages each dimension across all accepted candidates of the same skill family in the corresponding domain; is the number of accepted versions of that family across all evolution rounds. Three patterns appear across the three domains. First, executability is consistently the highestscoring dimension (0.901.00), reflecting the upstream effect of the executable validation gate (C.4): the teacher rarely sees code that does not import or returns wrong type, so it can score executability close to its ceiling. Second, trigger is the consistently lowest-scoring dimension (0.650.87), echoing the recurring difficulty of writing should_activate predicate that is neither too broad nor too narrow the teacher review prompt explicitly asks about both failure modes (Listing C.4). Third, two skill families dominate the per-family ranking across all three domains: reasoning_hallucination (Qskill = 0.89/0.90/0.91 on web/math/code) and premature_final (0.88/0.89/0.86). These are also the two families that contribute the largest validation uplift (reasoning_hallucination: +9.3 avg. points on web; Table 17), so high review scores correlate with downstream utility rather than being free credit. I.2 Per-Domain Filter Outcomes and Admission Counts Table 19 reports library-growth statistics across the three domains. The closed-loop runs span 5 epochs for web-search and 3 epochs each for math and code under the lite-evolve schedule of D.5; the proposer is rate-limited to at most five candidates per epoch (max_candidates_per_epoch, C.3), and the library is hard-capped at max_library_size = 50 active skills. Under the current audit-only acceptance policy of C.4C.5, all candidates that compile are appended to the library 36 Table 18: Per-family average review scores across all accepted candidates of each skill family. C/T/I/E/V are concept / trigger / intervention / executability / validation; Qskill is the weighted composite. Counts (n) are the total accepted versions over all rounds. Skill family T Qskill Web-search domain reasoning_hallucination premature_final no_read_before_final pf_override_harmful wrong_entity_focus Math domain reasoning_hallucination premature_final wrong_entity_focus format_mismatch Code domain reasoning_hallucination premature_final format_mismatch incomplete_implementation incomplete_logic wrong_entity_focus incomplete_solution incomplete_code 5 3 4 2 4 3 3 3 3 12 12 12 6 4 12 1 1 0.90 0.90 0.85 0.80 0.80 0.93 0.90 0.80 0. 0.94 0.88 0.78 0.80 0.72 0.78 0.80 0.80 0.80 0.80 0.72 0.70 0.78 0.83 0.83 0.67 0.67 0.87 0.83 0.68 0.70 0.65 0.67 0.70 0.70 0.92 0.83 0.85 0.90 0.72 0.87 0.87 0.77 0. 0.87 0.79 0.67 0.67 0.75 0.65 0.60 0.60 0.98 1.00 0.95 1.00 0.95 1.00 1.00 0.93 0.90 1.00 0.96 0.94 0.92 0.93 0.90 0.90 0.90 0.86 0.83 0.80 0.80 0.75 0.83 0.80 0.70 0. 0.83 0.81 0.68 0.70 0.65 0.68 0.70 0.70 0.89 0.88 0.84 0.84 0.80 0.90 0.89 0.78 0.74 0.91 0.86 0.76 0.76 0.74 0.74 0.74 0.74 with their review scores stored alongside, so that closed-loop policies can flip the gate on without re-running rollouts. Table 19: Library growth statistics across self-improvement rounds, by domain. Mean Qskill averages over accepted candidates; Min and Max report extremes. Under the strict Qskill 0.60 acceptance threshold of C.5, every accepted candidate would still be admitted (min Qskill 0.665 across all three domains). Domain Epochs Accepted Distinct families Mean Qskill Min Max Web-search Math Code 5 3 3 18 12 55 5 4 0.85 0.83 0.80 0.745 0.740 0.665 0.965 0.925 0.965 The web-search and math runs admit between 4 and 6 candidates per epoch, comfortably under the per-epoch cap; the code run admits more candidates per epoch ( 18) because its lite-evolve schedule (D.5) re-samples failed trajectories more aggressively and the proposer emits multiple variants per residual cluster. Even with this faster growth, the active library never exceeds the 50-skill hard cap, because each admission goes into versioned slot (__v{N}) and only the latest version of each base id (skill_ids_latest) is exposed at runtime. Older versions remain on disk for trajectory replay but never fire, so retrieval precision is bounded by the number of distinct skill families (5/4/8 across web/math/code) rather than the cumulative count of accepted versions. I.3 Filtering Outcomes Under the Strict-Gate Policy Although the current pipeline is in audit-only mode, the on-disk scores let us reconstruct what the strict-gate policy of C.5 would do. With Qexec 0.30 for hard rejection, Qskill 0.60 for acceptance, and Qskill 0.75 for new-category admission, all 85 candidates accepted across the three domains would still pass the basic acceptance threshold (min Qskill = 0.665 in code), but only 52 of 85 (61%) would clear the new-category bar of 0.75. The boundary cases concentrate in the families with the lowest review scores format_mismatch (math/code), wrong_entity_focus (code), incomplete_logic, and incomplete_implementation (code) which in our system are generally accepted as refinements of an existing seed family rather than new categories. This is consistent with the multi-tier threshold design: the framework lets weak refinement past the same-family bar while reserving the higher bar for genuinely new skill concepts. The same-family 37 bar is what controls library noise during rapid evolution, and it is the lever that the filtering ablation in Table 5 exercises directly. I.4 Skill-Family Evolution Pattern Across all three domains the evolved library converges on small set of recurring families that map onto the Phase failure-detector taxonomy (C.2): premature_final, no_read_before_final, reasoning_hallucination, wrong_entity_focus, and format_mismatch dominate the web-search and math libraries, and the same five plus incomplete_implementation/incomplete_logic dominate code. This convergence is informative: the proposer (the student vLLM) is choosing skill ids that overlap heavily with the heuristic detectors of Phase even though it sees the failure clusters as natural-language abstractions, not as named heuristics. In other words, the student rediscovers the failure taxonomy from data rather than copying it from labels which we read as sanity check that the closed-loop signal is not collapsing to the proposer reproducing fixed list. The cross-domain reuse of family names also explains why the lite-evolve mode is sufficient for E5/E6: most epochs add refinement of an already-named family rather than genuinely new concept, and the executable validation pipeline (C.4) is what keeps refinements compatible with the existing dispatcher. Compute, Hardware, and Reproducibility This section consolidates the resource requirements, hardware specifications, and reproducibilitycritical metadata for all experiments in the paper, in line with the NeurIPS reproducibility checklist. J.1 Hardware Configuration All training and evaluation were run on SLURM cluster constrained to NVIDIA l40s. All hardware allocations are summarized in Table 20. Memory is RSS only (no offload), and vLLM engines reuse the GPU when shared between rollout and proposal stages so that no double-load occurs. Table 20: Per-job hardware allocations. Wall-clock budgets are SLURM ceilings, not measured run-time; actual completion times are reported in Table 21. These numbers exclude (a) the one-shot bootstrap rollout (D.6, Layer 0) which runs once and amortizes across V1 settings, and (b) the post-training evaluation which reuses the skill-eval job above. Aggregating across all six post-training experiments, the bootstrap rollout, the three self-improving runs, and the eight skill-eval ablation rows in Table 5, the total compute footprint of the paper is on the order of 2,0002,500 GPU-hours on l40s-class hardware. 38 Table 21: Approximate wall-clock per post-training run. Step counts are TRL optimizer steps (logging_steps = 10); see for the canonical-run selection. Run Optimizer steps Approx. epochs Wall-clock (h) E1 V1 SFT E2 V1 RS E3 V1 OPD E4 V2 SFT E5 V2 RS E6 V2 OPD Bootstrap roll Self-improving (5 ep, web) Self-improving (3 ep, math/code) 600 1,400 900 820 + 180 700 + 360 280 + 140 10 8 2 10 2 iters 2 iters 2 iters 5 epochs 3 epochs 12 24 18 20 22 14 6 30 8/10 J.3 Teacher API Budget All teacher-augmented stages call gpt-4o (snapshot gpt-4o-2024-11-20) through the standard OpenAI Chat Completions API. We pinned this snapshot for all experiments to keep the teacher behavior reproducible across the papers lifetime; any future change to OpenAIs default gpt-4o alias would not affect the reported numbers but would affect re-runs unless the snapshot suffix is preserved. Approximate token counts per stage, aggregated across the full paper, are: 80 input + 10 output for skill-eval inference (across all eight ablation rows), 25 input + 5 output for the three self-improving loops (Phase AH total), and 20 input + 4 output for the on-policy distillation runs (E3, E6). The single most expensive stage is full evolve in E4 ( 1 per evolve cycle, two cycles), driven by the Phase teacher review of every candidate. Lite evolve in E5/E6 makes no teacher API calls. J.4 Random Seeds and Variance All training runs use seed = 42 (Hugging Face TrainingArguments.seed and Python random.seed) for the trainer, and separate fixed seed for the validation-pool shuffle in D.1. Rollout sampling under RS and OPD uses temperature 0.9 / top-p 0.95, so trajectory generation is non-deterministic; the variance across re-rollouts is bounded primarily by group size = 2 rather than by seed choice. We report all results from single seed because re-running E1E6 across multiple seeds is prohibitive ( 6 the compute budget); based on internal smoke tests with = 4 rollouts, the standard deviation of the web-search EM average is approximately 1.5 points across re-runs of the same seed, which is smaller than the gaps reported in Table 2. We flag the absence of multi-seed numbers as known limitation. J.5 Software, Code, and Checkpoint Release The codebase is implemented in Python (3.11) on top of transformers, trl, peft, and vllm; exact pinned versions are recorded in the repositorys requirements.txt. The skill library, generated PFs, library-history JSONs, and training-data jsonls live under self_improving/; the PF runtime is in src/skills_agent/skills/. Pre-trained LoRA adapters for E1E6 and the evolved skill libraries will be released with the camera-ready under permissive license; teacher API logs are not released because they contain raw third-party API responses, but the prompts that produce them are fully documented in Appendix (Boxes C.1C.4). The full validation/test split files generated under self_improving/data/ are deterministic given the source AgentFlow-aligned datasets, the seed boundary in Table 10, and seed = 42; we will release these as well to remove ambiguity. J.6 Reproducibility Checklist Pointers The reproducibility checklist questions map to specific appendix locations: dataset choice and splits are in D.1; full hyperparameters in Table 13 and D; rollout configuration in D; failure-summarizer 39 / proposer / reviewer prompts in Boxes C.1C.4; the evaluation protocol (per-dataset metrics, judges, and aggregations) in D.1; and qualitative trajectory comparisons in G.4.

sections.title: K Cost and Latency Breakdown
sections.content: The framework instantiates teacher model at up to 17 distinct invocation sites per episode (B.6). This section quantifies the per-episode token budget and approximate dollar cost of each setting in Table 5, so that practical adoption decisions can be made without recomputing the budget from first principles. All numbers refer to the web-search domain (which has the longest rollouts); math and code costs are roughly 3050% lower because rollouts collapse to single step. K.1 Per-Setting Cost Aggregates We estimate token cost using the OpenAI gpt-4o-2024-11-20 list price at the time of the experiments (input $2.50/M, output $10.00/M). Under the full HASP setting, the average cost is approximately $0.045 per question. We also report wall-clock time per episode (median over 200 samples), so readers can translate these costs into approximate API throughput if needed. The main cost comes from the eight FINAL-time LLM-assisted handlers (B.6), which together account for about 50% of teacher tokens per episode in the full setting. Mid-rollout PFs (retrieval_failure, format_extraction_error, reasoning_error, and answer_confidence_guard) are rate-limited and contribute about 10%. Pre-rollout components such as the Difficulty Gate and PFSelector cost about 2k tokens once per episode, so their relative cost becomes smaller on longer multi-step rollouts. Format post-processing is consistently about 0.5k tokens per FINAL action.
